Vascular Surgery Elective: 2-Week Survival & Performance Guide

Part

PART I, TIER 1: THE CRAM

▸

1. Master Numbers Card (the single source of truth: memorize cold)

Conflicts that scattered resources get wrong, resolved here once: ABI severe = <0.40 (not <0.3); ALI warm-ischemia window ≈ 6 h (collateral-dependent rule of thumb, not a hard cutoff); acceptable perioperative stroke/death is <6% for symptomatic carotid and <3% for asymptomatic, pair them so you never conflate them.

ABI / TBI: the single most-pimped vascular number

ABI = (higher ankle systolic, DP or PT, of that leg) ÷ (higher of the two brachial pressures).

ABI Meaning
> 1.40 Non-compressible (calcified, diabetes, ESRD) → ABI unreliable, get a TBI / toe pressure
1.00 – 1.40 Normal
0.91 – 0.99 Borderline
0.41 – 0.90 PAD / claudication range
< 0.40 Severe; rest pain / tissue loss / CLTI territory
  • TBI < 0.70 = abnormal (toe digital arteries usually escape calcification, so TBI works when ABI is falsely high).
  • Toe pressure < 30 mmHg (and ankle pressure < 50 mmHg) → poor wound healing / CLTI.
  • Exercise ABI: claudication symptoms with a normal resting ABI → a post-treadmill drop (≥ 20%, or ankle pressure fall ≥ 30 mmHg) confirms PAD.

AAA, thresholds, growth, screening, surveillance (why: UKSAT, ADAM, repair when rupture risk > operative risk)

  • AAA defined: infrarenal aorta ≥ 3.0 cm (≈ 1.5× normal).
  • Elective repair: ≥ 5.5 cm (men), ≥ 5.0 cm (women), OR growth > 0.5 cm / 6 mo (or > 1 cm/yr), OR symptomatic/tender, any size.
  • Screening (USPSTF): one-time US for men 65–75 who ever smoked (grade B); selectively offer never-smoker men (grade C); not recommended in never-smoking women (grade D).
  • Surveillance (SVS): 3.0–3.9 cm → ~ every 3 yr; 4.0–4.9 cm → every 12 mo; 5.0–5.4 cm → every 6 mo.
  • Ruptured AAA triad: hypotension + back/flank pain + pulsatile mass → permissive hypotension (SBP ~70–90 while awake), straight to OR/hybrid.
  • Laplace: wall tension ∝ radius × pressure, why rupture risk climbs steeply with diameter (the rationale for the size thresholds).

Carotid stenosis: thresholds, timing, and the paired risk cutoffs

  • Symptomatic (ipsilateral TIA / stroke / amaurosis fugax within 6 mo) + ≥ 50% → revascularize; benefit greatest 70–99% (NASCET/ECST). Operate within ~2 weeks of the event. Acceptable perioperative stroke/death < 6%.
  • Asymptomatic, classic teaching ≥ 70% if life expectancy > 3–5 yr and perioperative stroke/death < 3% (ACAS/ACST). But this has changed (see CREST-2 below).
  • Near-occlusion / "string sign" / chronic total occlusion → do NOT intervene.
  • NASCET method (residual lumen vs distal normal ICA) is standard; reads a lower % than ECST for the same lesion.
★ Must-know

CREST-2 (NEJM, Nov 2025), the single most current carotid point. In asymptomatic ≥ 70% stenosis, all on intensive medical therapy: stenting + intensive medical management reduced 4-yr stroke/death (2.8% vs 6.0%, P = 0.02), but CEA did NOT (3.7% vs 5.3%, P = 0.24). Bottom line to say out loud: intensive medical therapy is the floor, and routine CEA for asymptomatic disease is now hard to justify. The ≥ 70% symptomatic CEA indication is unchanged. This is the highest-value single thing to have loaded.

Heparin / ACT / protamine (you WILL be pimped on this)

  • Systemic heparin BEFORE clamping (prevents stagnant-column thrombosis). ~80–100 U/kg IV (many use a fixed ~5,000 U for routine peripheral); circulate ~3 min before clamping.
  • Monitor with ACT: baseline ~100–140 s; target ~250–300 s for vascular clamping (> 400–480 s for CPB). "Are we heparinized?" → the answer is the ACT.
  • Reverse with protamine: ~1 mg per 100 U of residual heparin. Risks: hypotension, bradycardia, anaphylaxis, higher in NPH/protamine-insulin users, fish allergy, prior exposure. Give slowly.

Dialysis access: Rule of 6s

A fistula is mature by ~6 weeks when: flow ≥ 600 mL/min, diameter ≥ 6 mm, depth ≤ 6 mm, with a ~6 cm cannulatable segment (KDOQI). Order: fistula > graft > catheter; distal-before-proximal, non-dominant arm first (radiocephalic → brachiocephalic → brachiobasilic transposition → graft → catheter).

Consolidated drug-dose box (so you're not hunting across sections)

Drug Dose / target Note
Heparin (intra-op) 80–100 U/kg, or fixed ~5,000 U before clamp; ACT 250–300 s
Protamine ~1 mg / 100 U residual heparin anaphylaxis risk
Aspirin 75–100 mg daily single antiplatelet for nearly all PAD
Clopidogrel 75 mg daily alternative antiplatelet (CAPRIE)
High-intensity statin atorvastatin 40–80 / rosuvastatin 20–40 regardless of baseline LDL; LDL goal < 70 (often < 55)
Cilostazol 100 mg BID claudication symptoms; contraindicated in any heart failure
Low-dose rivaroxaban + ASA rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + ASA "vascular dose" (COMPASS, VOYAGER-PAD); more bleeding
BP / glucose < 130/80; A1c ~ < 7% individualized

2. The Five Master Tables (one canonical copy of each)

Table A: CEA cranial nerves (this block doubles as your July ENT cranial-nerve exam)

Nerve Injury sign Where it's at risk
Hypoglossal (XII) Tongue deviates toward the lesion Crosses ICA/ECA high, ~2 cm above bifurcation
Vagus (X) / RLN fibers Hoarseness In the carotid sheath (posterior, between artery & IJV); most common cause of post-CEA hoarseness is vagal, not direct RLN
Superior laryngeal n. (external br.) Voice fatigue, loss of high pitch ("opera singer's nerve") Runs with the superior thyroid artery
Marginal mandibular br. (VII) Asymmetric lower-lip/smile High incision/retraction near the mandibular angle
Glossopharyngeal (IX) Dysphagia Very high ICA dissection
Spinal accessory (XI) Trapezius weakness, shoulder drop Posterior triangle, the neck-dissection nerve (ENT)
Ansa cervicalis Minimal (strap motor) On the anterior sheath; can be divided for high exposure

Carotid sheath = "VAN": Vein (IJV, lateral), Artery (carotid, medial), Nerve (vagus, posterior between them). Sympathetic chain behind → injury = Horner.

Table B: EVAR endoleaks (guaranteed pimp)

Type Source Pressure / management
I Seal-zone leak at proximal (Ia) / distal (Ib) attachment High pressure → treat now (balloon/cuff/extension)
II Retrograde branch backflow (lumbar, IMA), most common Usually low → observe; embolize if sac grows
III Graft defect / component separation High pressure → treat (reline)
IV Graft fabric porosity Transient/self-limited → observe
V Endotension, sac grows, no visible leak Surveillance ± reintervention
Pearl

Mnemonic: I and III = high-pressure, fix them; II = most common, leave it (unless sac grows); IV = porosity, transient; V = endotension/mystery.

Table C, Acute limb ischemia: 6 Ps + Rutherford (staging = treatment)

6 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia (cold), Paresthesia, Paralysis, the last two are the late, limb-threatening pair. First move in any ALI: IV heparin. Warm-ischemia window ≈ 6 h (collateral-dependent rule of thumb).

Class Sensory Motor Doppler (arterial / venous) Action
I Viable None None Both audible Not immediately threatened; image/plan
IIa Marginal Minimal (toes) None Arterial often inaudible / venous audible Salvageable, prompt Rx
IIb Immediate > toes, rest pain Mild–moderate Arterial inaudible / venous audible Salvage = emergent revascularization
III Irreversible Profound, anesthetic Profound, paralysis/rigor Both inaudible Primary amputation (do NOT reperfuse → rhabdo/hyperK)

Embolic vs thrombotic: embolic = sudden, AFib (#1), normal contralateral pulses, no claudication history, lodges at bifurcations (common femoral) → Fogarty embolectomy. Thrombotic = on prior PAD, gradual, abnormal other leg → lysis/bypass.

Table D: Ulcer differentiation

Feature Arterial Venous Neuropathic (diabetic)
Location Toes, dorsum, lateral foot, pressure points Medial malleolus (gaiter zone) Plantar pressure points (metatarsal heads, heel)
Pain Painful (worse on elevation) Mild/aching (better on elevation) Painless (neuropathy)
Edges/base Punched-out, dry, pale/necrotic Irregular, shallow, granulating; hemosiderin Punched-out, callused rim
Pulses Absent/diminished Present Often present (check, can be neuro-ischemic)
Key Rx Revascularize Compression (after excluding arterial) Offloading + glucose control + debridement

Table E, CLTI revascularization: BEST-CLI vs BASIL-2 (reconcile, don't memorize as a contradiction)

Trial Population Finding
BEST-CLI (NEJM 2022) CLTI with an adequate single-segment great saphenous vein Open bypass beat endovascular (fewer MALE/death); no good vein → the two were similar
BASIL-2 (Lancet 2023) CLTI needing infrapopliteal revascularization Endovascular-first gave better amputation-free survival (largely fewer deaths)
Pearl

The reconciliation (say it in one breath): conduit quality + disease level decide, good vein + a more proximal target → bypass; tibial-level disease (especially frailer patients) → endo-first. Not "open always wins." Attendings test the reconciliation, not the trials individually.

3. Before Monday: This-Weekend Cram (3–4 hrs, already prioritized)

  • A. Top anatomy (45 min): master two maps cold: the carotid bifurcation (Part II §1) and the lower-extremity arterial tree (Part II §2). Add the femoral triangle (NAVEL, lateral→medial: Nerve, Artery, Vein, Empty space, Lymphatics; nerve sits outside the sheath), the aortic branch levels, and the pulse-exam sites.
  • B. Top 5 cases, one line each (45 min): EVAR, CEA, lower-extremity bypass, peripheral angiogram ± angioplasty/stent, AV fistula. For each: indication / key anatomy / what it does / your role (full version in Part III).
  • C. Exam + ABI + OR etiquette (60–90 min): drill the ABI table and how to take one; practice the vascular exam on a friend; lock the OR-etiquette basics (Part IV §3). Tonight: one knot-tying video (two-handed + instrument tie) + one CEA or EVAR animation, then 20 knots on a chair rail.
  • D. Sunday-night distillation, say these out loud:
    • ICA has no neck branches; 1st ECA branch = superior thyroid. Arch order: innominate → L CCA → L subclavian.
    • Aorta levels: celiac T12, SMA L1, renals L1–2, IMA L3, bifurcation L4.
    • Leg chain: CFA → profunda + SFA → popliteal → AT + tibioperoneal trunk (→ PT + peroneal). SFA at Hunter's canal = #1 occlusion; AT comes off above the TP trunk.
    • ABI ≤ 0.90 = PAD; < 0.40 = severe. > 1.40 → TBI.
    • AAA: 5.5 (men) / 5.0 (women). Symptomatic carotid ≥ 70% → CEA, < 2 wk. Asymptomatic: CREST-2 (2025), meds are the floor; stenting reduced events, CEA did not.
    • CLTI: BEST-CLI (good vein → bypass) vs BASIL-2 (below-knee → endo-first). Name both.
    • 6 Ps; Rutherford IIb = revascularize now, III = amputate; first move = heparin.
    • Endoleak I & III treat; II most common (observe).
    • Heparin before clamp, ACT 250–300, protamine 1 mg/100 U.
Part

PART II, TIER 2 REFERENCE: ANATOMY TO KNOW COLD

▸

1. Carotid bifurcation: the single highest-yield region (and pure head & neck) [ENT]

  • CCA bifurcates at the upper border of the thyroid cartilage ≈ C3–C4 (variable, C3–C5; don't quote one level as gospel). ICA posterolateral, ECA anteromedial.
  • Carotid bulb = proximal ICA = plaque site; seat of the carotid sinus (baroreceptor, CN IX) and carotid body (chemoreceptor, CN IX).
  • Two guaranteed pimps: First ECA branch? → superior thyroid artery. ICA branches in the neck? → zero (first is the ophthalmic, intracranial). On angio, the branchy neck vessel is the ECA; the smooth one to the skull base is the ICA.
  • ECA branches, "Some Anatomists Like Freaking Out Poor Medical Students": Superior thyroid, Ascending pharyngeal, Lingual, Facial, Occipital, Posterior auricular, Maxillary, Superficial temporal. Maxillary → middle meningeal (epidural hematoma) + sphenopalatine = "artery of epistaxis" [ENT].
  • ICA vs ECA on duplex: ICA = low-resistance waveform (high diastolic flow, feeds brain); ECA = high-resistance (sharp systole, little diastole) + temporal tap confirms ECA.
  • Cranial nerves at the carotid → Master Table A. Carotid sheath = VAN.
  • Why the L/R RLN asymmetry matters: left RLN loops under the aortic arch (long mediastinal course) → a left mediastinal/aortic process causes hoarseness ("cardiovocal/Ortner syndrome"). Right RLN loops under the right subclavian. [ENT]

2. Lower-extremity arterial tree: the bread and butter

Aorta (L4)
 → Common iliac → Internal iliac (hypogastric) [pelvis/buttock]
                → External iliac → (under inguinal ligament) →
   Common femoral artery (CFA)
     → Profunda femoris (deep) [KEY collateral]
     → Superficial femoral artery (SFA) → (Hunter's/adductor canal) →
        Popliteal → Anterior tibial (→ dorsalis pedis)
                  → Tibioperoneal trunk → Posterior tibial + Peroneal
Point High-yield why
CFA The access vessel, puncture below the inguinal ligament, over the femoral head (compressible against bone). Too high → retroperitoneal bleed; too low → pseudoaneurysm/AV fistula.
Profunda femoris Main collateral when the SFA occludes; profundaplasty is a real operation.
SFA #1 site of lower-extremity occlusion, classically at Hunter's (adductor) canal (contains femoral vessels + saphenous nerve).
Popliteal #1 peripheral artery aneurysm; bilateral ~50%; ~⅓–½ have a coexisting AAA. Presents with thrombosis/distal embolism, rarely rupture.
Tibioperoneal trunk Splits into PT + peroneal; AT comes off the popliteal ABOVE the trunk.

Pulse sites: femoral (mid-inguinal point), popliteal (deep, knee flexed), dorsalis pedis (lateral to EHL tendon; congenitally absent ~2–10%), posterior tibial (behind medial malleolus). Disease patterns: buttock/hip/thigh claudication = aortoiliac; calf = femoropopliteal; foot = tibial/pedal ("claudication is one level above the symptom"). Diabetes → infrapopliteal/tibial; tobacco → aortoiliac/femoropopliteal.

3. Aorta, arch & viscera: "name the vessel on CTA"

  • Wall layers: intima (atherosclerosis starts; dissection = intimal tear) / media (elastin/SMC; aneurysm = media weakening) / adventitia (vasa vasorum, holds suture). True aneurysm = all 3 layers; pseudoaneurysm = contained rupture, NOT all layers (puncture-site complication).
  • Abdominal aorta branches: celiac T12 (L gastric, splenic, common hepatic), SMA L1, renals L1–2, IMA L3, bifurcation L4 (≈ umbilicus), IVC confluence L5. Foregut/midgut/hindgut = celiac/SMA/IMA.
  • Mesenteric collaterals: marginal artery of Drummond (peripheral arcade) + arc of Riolan (central, "meandering mesenteric") connect SMA↔︎IMA; a prominent arc on angio signals significant occlusive disease. Watershed: splenic flexure (Griffiths) and rectosigmoid (Sudeck).
  • Aortic arch order: brachiocephalic (innominate) → left CCA → left subclavian ("ABC"). Bovine arch (~13–15%) = most common variant. Aberrant right subclavian (arteria lusoria) → dysphagia lusoria + non-recurrent right inferior laryngeal nerve (classic thyroid-surgery trap) [ENT]. Subclavian steal: proximal subclavian stenosis (before the vertebral) → retrograde vertebral flow on arm use; inter-arm SBP difference ≥ 15 mmHg.
  • Spinal cord supply: artery of Adamkiewicz (T8–L1, left ~75%) → anterior spinal artery; risk of paraplegia after thoracoabdominal repair → CSF drainage + MAP augmentation.

4. Upper-extremity, access & venous anatomy

  • Arm arteries: subclavian → (lateral 1st rib) axillary → (teres major) brachial → radial + ulnar (antecubital). Allen test before radial access/harvest. Radial access is now default for many diagnostic caths.
  • Access veins: cephalic (lateral, → radiocephalic/brachiocephalic fistula), basilic (medial, deep → must transpose), median cubital (classic venipuncture).
  • GSV: longest vein; anterior to the medial malleolus (cutdown landmark), up the medial leg → common femoral vein at the saphenofemoral junction. #1 bypass conduit.
  • SSV: posterior calf, behind lateral malleolus → popliteal vein.
  • Deep veins parallel the named arteries and are the DVT/PE source. Trap: the old "superficial femoral vein" is a DEEP vein, anticoagulate it.
  • CEAP grades chronic venous disease (C6 = active ulcer). May-Thurner: left common iliac vein compressed by the right common iliac artery → left-leg DVT. Varicose veins: endovenous thermal ablation has replaced open stripping.
Part

PART III, TIER 2 REFERENCE: THE 10 CASES & THE WARDS

▸

A. The 10 cases (indication → anatomy → operation → one trial)

Tier-1 five (you WILL see and be pimped on):

  1. AAA, open vs EVAR. Indication: see card. Anatomy: ~95% infrarenal; the neck (normal aorta between lowest renal and aneurysm) must seat an EVAR graft; left renal vein crosses anterior to the aorta (retroaortic ~2–3% = bleeding trap); IMA sacrifice risks sigmoid ischemia. Operation: open = clamp + sew graft (transperitoneal vs retroperitoneal); EVAR = femoral stent-graft. Trial: EVAR-1/DREAM, EVAR lower early mortality, curves converge ~2–3 yr, late sac-rupture risk → lifelong surveillance. Most early death after repair = cardiac (MI); feared bowel complication = ischemic colitis (sigmoid).
  2. Carotid stenosis, CEA vs CAS. Indication/timing/trials: see card + CREST-2. Operation: CEA = arteriotomy, remove plaque + diseased intima, patch closure (reduces restenosis/stroke) or eversion; selective shunt if back/stump pressure < 40–50 mmHg, EEG/SSEP change, or awake-patient deficit. Release ECA/CCA first, ICA last (flush debris away from the brain). CAS/TCAR favored for hostile/irradiated neck ([ENT] field logic), high lesion, younger patient.
  3. PAD, claudication vs CLTI. Claudication: medical + supervised exercise FIRST (cilostazol, statin, antiplatelet, smoking cessation; CLEVER, exercise matched stenting for walking). CLTI (Rutherford 4–6): revascularize. Trials: BEST-CLI / BASIL-2 (Table E). Conduit: single-segment ipsilateral GSV is gold; prosthetic does poorly below the knee.
  4. Acute limb ischemia. 6 Ps, Rutherford, embolic vs thrombotic, heparin first → see Table C. Watch reperfusion/compartment syndrome (pain on passive stretch early, pulselessness late) → fasciotomy; monitor CK/K⁺/myoglobin.
  5. Hemodialysis access. Fistula-first, Rule of 6s → see card. Complications: steal syndrome (DRIL/banding), thrombosis/stenosis, aneurysm, high-output failure. Allen test first; avoid subclavian lines (central stenosis ruins future access).

Tier-2/3 (common and pimpable):

  1. Lower-extremity bypass, name it by endpoints (fem-pop, fem-distal, fem-fem, ax-fem). Above-knee popliteal tolerates prosthetic; below-knee demands vein (reversed or in-situ). Fix inflow before any distal bypass.
  2. Aortoiliac occlusive disease (Leriche). Triad: buttock claudication + erectile dysfunction + absent femoral pulses. Endo-first (kissing iliac stents); open gold standard = aortobifemoral bypass (~80–90% 5-yr patency); extra-anatomic (ax-fem, fem-fem) for high-risk/hostile abdomen.
  3. Mesenteric ischemia. Acute: "pain out of proportion to exam," SMA embolism (#1, AFib), lactate rises late (normal lactate doesn't exclude it), CTA → revascularize + resect + second-look. Chronic: postprandial pain, food fear, weight loss; usually ≥ 2 of 3 vessels; endo stent first-line. NOMI = low-flow/pressors.
  4. Amputations. Most distal level that will heal: toe/ray → TMA → BKA (best function, preserve the knee) → AKA (heals more reliably). Healing predictors: TcPO₂ > 40, toe pressure > 30. Revascularize first if it lets you go more distal.
  5. Varicose veins / CVI. Valve incompetence → reflux (> 0.5 s on duplex) → venous hypertension. CEAP (C6 = active ulcer). Ladder: compression + elevation → endovenous ablation (RFA/laser) → sclerotherapy/phlebectomy.

B. The focused vascular exam (do this on every patient, every day)

  • Pulse grading 0–2+: 0 absent, 1+ diminished, 2+ normal (confirm your team's scale). Document palpable vs Doppler separately, a pulse you only hear is "Doppler signal, monophasic," not 1+. Check carotid, brachial, radial/ulnar, aorta, femoral, popliteal, DP and PT (the pedal pulses are the money).
  • Doppler waveform: triphasic = normal; biphasic = mild disease (or normal variant); monophasic = significant proximal stenosis; absent = no flow.
  • Bruit = turbulence ≈ stenosis (carotid bruit correlates poorly with severity; a near-occlusion can be silent). A thrill/bruit over a dialysis access is normal, loss of thrill = it clotted.
  • Acute limb sensorimotor exam stages ALI (light touch between toes goes first; then motor) → Table C.
  • Diabetic foot: neuropathy (10-g monofilament), ischemia (medial calcinosis → non-compressible vessels), infection (probe-to-bone + → osteomyelitis). Shoes AND socks off, look between every toe. WIfI (Wound, Ischemia, foot Infection) stratifies amputation risk.

C. Imaging roles

Duplex (first-line, bedside, no contrast, carotid, AAA, graft/access surveillance, DVT) → CTA (the endovascular workhorse, AAA sizing, dissection, runoff; iodinated contrast) → catheter angiography/DSA (gold standard + therapeutic). MRA when contrast/renal is a concern (tends to overestimate stenosis). Rough flow: duplex to screen → CTA to plan → angiography to fix.

D. Taking a vascular consult (page-to-plan)

Get onset/laterality/pain, the exam (pulses + Doppler + sensorimotor), imaging, renal function/anticoagulation status, goals of care. Then triage by syndrome:

  • ALI (the can't-miss): viability → heparin bolus + drip now, NPO, call the team, CTA if it won't delay the OR. → Table C.
  • AAA/rupture: thresholds + triad → card.
  • Access problems: thrombosed fistula (lost thrill), bleeding, steal, infection; post-cath groin: pseudoaneurysm (pulsatile mass + systolic bruit / to-and-fro Doppler → US-guided thrombin), retroperitoneal bleed (high stick → flank pain + falling Hgb), AV fistula.
  • Non-healing ulcer: characterize (Table D) + ABI/TBI; the question is inflow enough to heal, or revascularize first?
  • Carotid TIA/stroke: amaurosis fugax / contralateral deficit / aphasia → duplex + CTA → card thresholds.
  • Mesenteric: acute "pain out of proportion"; chronic postprandial triad.

E. Post-op checks: "Is it still flowing?"

Check pulses/Doppler distal to any repair or access, compared to the documented baseline and the other side (a signal lost since PACU = emergency, page now). Inspect access/graft sites (hematoma, pulsatile mass, bleeding). Dialysis access: palpate thrill, auscultate bruit. After CEA: focused neuro + expanding neck hematoma (airway emergency, open at bedside; same logic as a post-thyroidectomy hematoma [ENT]), cranial-nerve checks (Table A), and labile BP → control to prevent cerebral hyperperfusion (ipsilateral headache, seizure, hemorrhage).

F. Medical management: the "vascular bundle" (pimped constantly)

Every atherosclerosis patient: single antiplatelet + high-intensity statin + smoking cessation + BP < 130/80 + glucose control; cilostazol for claudication (not in HF); supervised exercise first-line. Low-dose rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + ASA (COMPASS/VOYAGER-PAD) in select high-risk. Antiplatelet vs anticoagulation: arterial/atherosclerosis/stents/"white clot" → antiplatelet; cardioembolic (AFib)/VTE/"red clot" → anticoagulation; ALI → therapeutic heparin regardless.

G. The vascular presentation (look polished, ~30–45 s)

  1. One-liner: age/sex, key vascular history, procedure/POD#, "68M POD#2 from a right fem-pop bypass for CLTI with tissue loss."
  2. Overnight events; vitals (BP especially), I/Os, lines/drains.
  3. The vascular exam out loud, the money line: "Right DP and PT 1+ by Doppler, monophasic; graft site clean/dry/intact; foot warm, sensation intact, wound granulating."
  4. Relevant labs (Hgb, Cr, anticoagulation level). Lead with your assessment, then plan by problem. Daily note adds: bilateral pulse/Doppler exam, wound/access/graft, anticoagulation plan, foot exam in any diabetic/CLTI patient.
Part

PART IV, TIER 2 REFERENCE: OR SURVIVAL & THE ENT-AI REBUILD

▸

1. The five behaviors that ARE the eval (100% in your control Monday)

Job How to do it well
Suction Suck at the surgeon's instrument tip, not where you think the blood is; keep it still in a deep field; don't dab the anastomosis. A dry field is the whole game (vascular and ENT).
Follow suture Gentle constant tension; release exactly as the needle pulls through; never yank (tears intima) or go slack (loose anastomosis).
Cut suture Cut on the flat: lay scissor tips on the suture, slide to the knot, rotate ~45°, cut. Ask "how long?" once. Prolene tails ~2–3 mm.
Retract Hold still in their vector; don't readjust/peek/lean. You are a human self-retainer.
Load needles / keep dry Load ~⅔ back from the tip, ~90° to the driver, hand back-loaded for their forehand; anticipate lap/irrigation/wipe the camera.

Behavioral wins: early, pre-rounded, knows the patients and their imaging; introduces self to scrub tech + circulator by name; gloves/gowns clean; "where do you want me?" once, then watch the field not the screen. Don't ask to throw a stitch until you've earned the field.

2. Endovascular workflow (you'll be scrubbed at a groin doing this)

  • Access = Seldinger (needle → wire → sheath; wire never leaves the body). US-guided CFA puncture over the femoral head (compressible → avoids retroperitoneal bleed from a high stick).
  • French sizing: 1 Fr = ⅓ mm; sheaths sized by inner diameter (outer is ~1.5–2 Fr larger, a 5 Fr sheath makes ~a 7 Fr hole). Big-bore EVAR (14–24 Fr) → percutaneous "pre-close" (two Perclose ProGlide) or cutdown.
  • Hold pressure at the end ~3 min per French (longer if anticoagulated). CO₂ angiography substitutes for contrast in renal failure (contraindicated above the diaphragm).

3. OR etiquette & the sterile field (re-sharpen the basics)

  • Sterile zone = front of gown chest-to-waist (table level) + sleeves to ~2 in above the elbow. Below the waist and above the chest are NOT sterile. Hands above waist, below shoulders, in front, where you can see them.
  • If you don't know it's sterile, it isn't, say so (calling your own break is respected). Don't reach across the field; pass back-to-back; face the field.
  • Closed gloving is the default self-gloving method and the classic rusty-student tell, re-drill it; have the scrub tech check you on day 1.
  • Hybrid/endo room: lead apron + dosimeter before you scrub (you can't get one after); step back during fluoro (dose falls with the square of distance).
  • Introduce yourself (8-sec script): "Hi, I'm [Name], the student with the team, okay if I scrub in? Where would you like me?" Learn the tech's and circulator's names. Never talk during the time-out.

4. Instruments, suture & the anastomosis

Named instruments: DeBakey (atraumatic pickups, never grab vessel with toothed forceps), bulldog (soft branch occlusion), Satinsky (side-biting partial occlusion), aortic cross-clamp (the "clamp time" clock), Fogarty (balloon embolectomy, 2–3 Fr tibial, 3–4 Fr fem-pop, 4–6 Fr aortoiliac), Potts scissors (extend the arteriotomy), Castroviejo scissors/driver (micro, you've seen these in ENT/micro labs [ENT]), Pruitt-Inahara (two-balloon carotid shunt) / Javid (clamp-held shunt), tunneler, vessel loops (single = retract, double = occlude), Rummel tourniquet, self-retainers (Weitlaner/Gelpi/Bookwalter).

Suture, direct ENT free-flap transfer [ENT]: Prolene = permanent monofilament, tapered CV needle (permanent because the anastomosis sees arterial pressure forever; monofilament = low drag/low infection). Sizes: 3-0/4-0 aorta, 5-0 femoral, 6-0 carotid, 7-0+ distal (8-0/9-0–10-0 free flap). Double-armed + parachute the posterior wall; forehand one wall, backhand the other; lay throws flat, constant tension; a surgeon's throw helps slick monofilament hold.

Anastomosis sequence to anticipate: heparinize → clamp → arteriotomy (#11) + extend (Potts) → (CEA: endarterectomy plane, tack distal intima, shunt if needed) → double-armed Prolene, parachute posterior wall, run each wall, back-bleed before final tie → release clamps (CEA: ECA/CCA first, ICA last) → confirm flow (Doppler/completion angiogram) → protamine → close.

5. Complications by case (the pimp money)

  • CEA: perioperative stroke; cranial-nerve injury (Table A); expanding neck hematoma → open at bedside (airway) [ENT]; cerebral hyperperfusion.
  • ALI/reperfusion: compartment syndrome (pain on passive stretch early, pulseless late), rhabdo/hyperK → fasciotomy.
  • Graft: early thrombosis = technical (kink/flap); late = neointimal hyperplasia/disease. Infection organisms: S. aureus early, S. epidermidis late.
  • EVAR: endoleaks (Table B).
  • Access: pseudoaneurysm, retroperitoneal hematoma (high stick), AV fistula, distal atheroembolism ("trash foot"/blue toe, mottled toes with palpable pulses).
  • AV access: steal syndrome → DRIL/banding.
  • DVT extreme: phlegmasia cerulea dolens (massive iliofemoral DVT, cyanotic limb → venous gangrene).

6. Knot-tying & suturing: the home-practice plan (your biggest July lever)

Buy/borrow a kit tonight (board or cabinet handle + suture, silicone pad, needle driver, Adsons, scissors; ~$25–40; ask residents for expired suture).

Week 1, knots (priority order): (1) two-handed square knot first and cold (opposite directions each throw; same direction = a granny that slips); (2) one-handed tie (both hands, the one most likely to be pimped); (3) instrument tie (skin/short suture; lay throws flat in opposite directions; surgeon's throw = double-wrap first throw); (4) two-hand deep tie into a cup/jar (simulate a deep neck/vessel field). Goal by end of Week 1: a flat, square, snug knot, 5+ throws, without looking, in < 15 s.

Week 2, needle handling on the pad: load the needle in its body, ~½–⅔ back, perpendicular; drive along its curve (supinate, don't push straight); simple interrupted (perpendicular entry, equal bites, evert, knot to the side); vertical mattress (far-far-near-near); subcuticular running (the cosmesis closure that transfers to a neck/facelift incision [ENT]); atraumatic forceps (grab dermis, never crush skin).

Nightly 20-min block: 10 min knots, 5 min needle loading/driving, 5 min one closure on the pad, do it tired, in low light, while talking.

7. The two genuine vascular→ENT transfer clusters [ENT]

  1. The carotid/neck block, the CEA exposure is a neck dissection: carotid bifurcation, ICA/ECA, the cranial nerves (Table A), the sheath (VAN), the IJV (free-flap recipient vein), the common facial vein (ligated for exposure). Microvascular free flaps are shared turf: radial-forearm/fibula/ALT flaps anastomose to ECA branches (facial, superior thyroid) with jugular venous outflow.
  2. Fine-tissue & Prolene skill, atraumatic handling under loupes/scope, slick monofilament technique (lay flat, constant tension, no-touch intima), suction-as-a-skill (clear the field without obscuring the surgeon's view), the exact skills July rewards.

8. Asking for graduated responsibility (so July feels familiar)

Earn each rung, ask in a calm moment (never mid-anastomosis): camera/suction (day 1–2) → retract → cut sutures → first-assist (the most valuable rung) → throw a few ties → close skin. Phrase as readiness, not entitlement: "I've been practicing knots every night, okay if I close part of this?" Accept "not this time" gracefully, ask next case, and after you do it ask "what would you change about that throw?" (the coachability signal that earns the next, bigger task).

Part

PART V, SELF-TEST: PIMP Q&A

▸

Quiz mode is on: tap a blurred answer to reveal it.

Cover the answer column to self-test; the ★ ten are the highest-yield.

The ★ Top 10

# Question Answer
★1 6 Ps of acute limb ischemia? Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia, Paresthesia, Paralysis (last two = late → IIb, operate now)
★2 ABI cutoffs? ≤ 0.90 PAD; 0.41–0.90 claudication; < 0.40 severe; > 1.40 non-compressible → TBI (< 0.70 abnormal)
★3 AAA repair size? ≥ 5.5 cm men / ≥ 5.0 cm women, or > 0.5 cm/6 mo, or symptomatic
★4 Symptomatic carotid, when/timing/risk? ≥ 50% (best 70–99%), within 2 wk, periop stroke/death < 6% (NASCET)
★5 Asymptomatic carotid? Classic ≥ 70% if periop risk < 3%; CREST-2 (2025): meds are the floor, stenting reduced events, CEA did not
★6 Why heparin before clamping? Prevents stagnant-column thrombosis; circulate ~3 min first
★7 Why Prolene? Permanent monofilament (low drag/infection); anastomosis under arterial pressure forever
★8 First branch of the ECA? Superior thyroid artery (SALFOPMS)
★9 Endoleak types to fix? I and III (high pressure); II most common (observe)
★10 What does every PAD patient get? High-intensity statin + single antiplatelet (PAD = coronary risk equivalent)

Anatomy

Q A
ICA branches in the neck? None (first = ophthalmic, intracranial)
Carotid sheath contents? VAN: carotid (medial), IJV (lateral), vagus (posterior between them)
Branches of the popliteal? Genicular → AT + tibioperoneal trunk (→ PT + peroneal)
Where does the GSV drain? Common femoral vein at the saphenofemoral junction; lies anterior to the medial malleolus
ICA vs ECA on duplex? ICA = low-resistance (high diastole), no branches; ECA = high-resistance + temporal tap
Carotid bifurcation level? ~ C3–C4 (variable)

Hemodynamics

Q A
Why does claudication happen? Fixed stenosis: rest supply OK, exercise demand outstrips it → reproducible ischemic pain relieved by rest
Rest pain location/relief? Forefoot; relieved by dependency (hanging the foot off the bed)
Buttock claudication + ED + absent femoral pulses? Leriche (aortoiliac)
Arc of Riolan? Central SMA↔︎IMA collateral (Drummond = peripheral arcade)
Why is ABI unreliable > 1.40? Mönckeberg medial calcinosis → non-compressible; use TBI
Subclavian steal? Proximal subclavian stenosis → retrograde vertebral flow; inter-arm SBP Δ ≥ 15 mmHg

Indications / thresholds

Q A
AAA surveillance intervals? 3.0–3.9 ~q3yr; 4.0–4.9 q12mo; 5.0–5.4 q6mo
EVAR vs open trade-off? EVAR lower early mortality; curves converge ~2–3 yr; lifelong surveillance
Who gets a carotid stent? High surgical risk / hostile (irradiated) neck / high lesion; TCAR for high-risk
What counts as "symptomatic" carotid? Ipsilateral TIA / stroke / amaurosis fugax within 6 mo
Rule of 6s? flow ≥ 600 mL/min, ≥ 6 mm, ≤ 6 mm deep, ~6 wk
Mesenteric ischemia, acute vs chronic? Acute: pain out of proportion, SMA embolus; chronic: postprandial pain + food fear + weight loss, ≥ 2 vessels

Operative / endovascular

Q A
Reverse heparin, dose? Protamine ~1 mg / 100 U residual; watch anaphylaxis
ACT target? ~250–300 s (> 400–480 CPB)
When do you shunt in CEA? Selective: back/stump pressure < 40–50 mmHg, EEG/SSEP change, or awake deficit
Why patch a CEA? Reduces restenosis + stroke vs primary closure
Clamp-release order in CEA? ECA/CCA first, ICA last (debris away from brain)
Spinal cord ischemia after aortic repair, artery? Adamkiewicz → CSF drain + MAP support
Most common early death after AAA repair? Cardiac (MI)

Management

Q A
Rutherford IIb vs III? IIb = motor/sensory deficit but salvageable (revascularize); III = irreversible → amputate
First step in ALI? IV heparin
Best single intervention for claudication? Smoking cessation + supervised exercise
Cilostazol contraindication? Heart failure
Normal Doppler waveform? Triphasic
"Pain out of proportion to exam"? Acute mesenteric ischemia
Post-CEA emergency to fear? Expanding neck hematoma (airway)
Trash foot / blue toe? Atheroembolism, mottled toes with palpable pulses

Trial cheat-lines (state the number AND the trial)

  • NASCET/ECST → symptomatic carotid (70–99% biggest benefit, down to 50%, < 2 wk).
  • ACAS/ACST → asymptomatic CEA benefit in the pre-statin era.
  • CREST → CEA vs CAS: equivalent composite; stent → more stroke, CEA → more MI; younger favor stent, older favor CEA.
  • CREST-2 (2025) → asymptomatic ≥ 70% on intensive meds: stenting reduced stroke, CEA did not.
  • EVAR-1/DREAM → EVAR lower early mortality, converges ~2–3 yr.
  • UKSAT/ADAM → no benefit repairing small AAA (4.0–5.5 cm).
  • BEST-CLI (2022) → good single-segment GSV → bypass beats endo.
  • BASIL-2 (2023) → infrapopliteal → endo-first better amputation-free survival.
  • COMPASS/VOYAGER-PAD → low-dose rivaroxaban + ASA reduces limb/cardiac events.
  • CLEVER → supervised exercise matched stenting for claudication walking distance.
Part

PART VI: THE 2-WEEK PLAN

▸

Daily rhythm (every working day)

Block What you do
Pre-round (~5:30–6:15a) See YOUR patients first: overnight events, vitals/I&Os, pulse + Doppler distal to any intervention, wound/access check, labs (Cr, Hgb), pain, diet, lines/drains. Have a one-liner + plan.
Rounds Present crisply, lead with the vascular exam (Part III §G). Know the foot.
OR / procedures Lead apron on for endo. Read tomorrow's case the night before. Position, retract, anticipate, suction, cut. Volunteer to close/vein-harvest as trust builds.
Clinic Gold for ABIs, duplex, wound checks, claudication-vs-CLTI decisions.
Evening (30–45 min) (1) read tomorrow's case; (2) one topic from the sequence below; (3) one targeted UpToDate skim if a patient prompts it. Stop at 45 min.

Read-the-night-before rule (drives most OR pimping success): for each case, in ~10 min know indication, the relevant anatomy/branches, the steps (open) or access-wire-sheath-device sequence (endo), the named complication, and one trial. Priority when stretched: your sick/post-op patients > your assigned case > clinic > extra OR. Each morning ask your resident: "What do you need from me, and which case is mine?" Never leave the floor uncovered to disappear into a case.

The 10-day topic sequence (highest-frequency first; let the OR list reorder it)

Day Evening topic Pair with
1 (Mon) PAD + ABI + claudication staging re-drill the ABI table
2 AAA: thresholds, screening, EVAR vs open aortic/iliac anatomy
3 Carotid + CEA/CAS (NASCET, CREST, CREST-2) carotid + cranial-nerve anatomy [ENT]
4 Acute limb ischemia + embolectomy Fogarty, heparin
5 LE bypass + endo PAD (BEST-CLI vs BASIL-2) fem/pop/tibial anatomy, conduits
6 CLTI + diabetic foot + ulcer types (WIfI) arterial vs venous ulcer
7 Dialysis access (Rule of 6s, steal) upper-extremity venous anatomy
8 Venous disease + VTE (CEAP, May-Thurner, IVC filters) superficial vs deep venous
9 Mesenteric & renovascular visceral vessels (celiac/SMA/IMA)
10 (Fri wk2) Endovascular fundamentals + periop meds review weak spots

Weekly checkpoints

  • End of Week 1: Can I rattle off ABI, AAA, carotid cutoffs, the 6 Ps, Fontaine/Rutherford without looking? Have I scrubbed an EVAR/angio and a CEA or bypass (if a category's missing, ask)? Am I reliably knowing my patients' distal pulses before rounds? Pick one thing to fix for Week 2.
  • Mid-Week 2 (Wed): ask for specific feedback ("what can I do to be more helpful, and what should I read up on?") and act on it visibly.
  • End of elective: make sure the attending knows your name and that you're going into ENT, frame your carotid/neck fluency as the bridge. Ask early if you'll need an evaluation/letter.

Resource list (one primary text, no bloat)

Resource Use it for How much
Pestana's Surgery Notes (vascular) fastest high-yield framework; thresholds/buzzwords once this weekend
One focused chapter (Sabiston/Schwartz vascular, or Mont Reid) one coherent read of the field skim Week 1
UpToDate, targeted topics only "clinical features/dx of PAD," "management of claudication / AAA," "CEA," "approach to acute limb ischemia" the night before the relevant case
Rutherford's Vascular Surgery selective reference only, the case you're scrubbing 10-min lookups
Knot-tying / suturing video one good two-handed + instrument-tie video this weekend + 5 min/night
CEA + EVAR animation visualize the steps night before that case

Skip: comprehensive textbooks cover-to-cover, low-yield embryology, every trial's subgroup data. You're building useful fluency in a field you're passing through, not board mastery.

The daily "rebuild" micro-habit checklist (tape it to your badge)

Morning

  • Arrived early, pre-rounded with a plan for each patient
  • Pulled up imaging + read today's cases
  • List updated; anticoagulation status known for each
  • Lead apron + dosimeter ready if there's an endo case

In the OR

  • Introduced myself; learned tech + circulator names; asked where to stand
  • Closed-gloved cleanly; hands above waist all case
  • Anticipated one move before being asked
  • Asked for one rung up the responsibility ladder (calm moment)
  • Stayed silent during the time-out and critical steps

On the floor

  • Owned/closed the loop on ≥ 2 tasks
  • Gave one tight, assessment-first presentation
  • Applied one piece of feedback the same day

Night (20 min)

  • 10 min knots
  • 5 min needle loading + driving
  • 5 min one closure on the pad
  • Reviewed tomorrow's first case + one anatomy structure or threshold/trial
Part

PART VII: ABBREVIATIONS & TRIALS

▸

Abbreviations

Abbr Meaning
A1c hemoglobin A1c (≈3-month average glucose)
AAA abdominal aortic aneurysm
ABI ankle-brachial index
ACT activated clotting time
AFib atrial fibrillation
AKA above-knee amputation
ALI acute limb ischemia
AMI acute mesenteric ischemia
ASA aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid)
ASIS anterior superior iliac spine
AT anterior tibial (artery)
BKA below-knee amputation
BMT best medical therapy
BP blood pressure
CABG coronary artery bypass grafting
CAS carotid artery stenting
CCA common carotid artery
CEA carotid endarterectomy
CEAP Clinical–Etiology–Anatomy–Pathophysiology (venous classification)
CFA common femoral artery
CKD chronic kidney disease
CLTI chronic limb-threatening ischemia
CMI chronic mesenteric ischemia
CN cranial nerve
CPB cardiopulmonary bypass
CSF cerebrospinal fluid
CTA CT angiography
CVI chronic venous insufficiency
DP dorsalis pedis (pulse/artery)
DRIL distal revascularization–interval ligation
DSA digital subtraction angiography
DVT deep vein thrombosis
ECA external carotid artery
ED erectile dysfunction
EHL extensor hallucis longus (tendon)
ESRD end-stage renal disease
ESVS European Society for Vascular Surgery
EVAR endovascular aneurysm repair
Fr French (catheter/sheath size; 1 Fr = ⅓ mm)
GSV great saphenous vein
HF heart failure
I&Os intake and output
ICA internal carotid artery
IJV internal jugular vein
IMA inferior mesenteric artery
IMM intensive medical management
IV intravenous
IVC inferior vena cava
KDOQI Kidney Disease Outcomes Quality Initiative
LDA lines, drains, airways
LDL low-density lipoprotein (cholesterol)
LIMA left internal mammary artery
MACE major adverse cardiovascular events
MALE major adverse limb events
MAP mean arterial pressure
MI myocardial infarction
MRA MR angiography
NAVEL Nerve, Artery, Vein, Empty space, Lymphatics (femoral triangle, lateral→medial)
NNT number needed to treat
NOMI nonocclusive mesenteric ischemia
NPH neutral protamine Hagedorn (insulin)
NPO nil per os (nothing by mouth)
OR operating room
PAD peripheral arterial disease
PDE3 phosphodiesterase-3 (cilostazol's drug class)
PE pulmonary embolism
POD postoperative day
PSV peak systolic velocity
PT posterior tibial (pulse/artery)
PTFE polytetrafluoroethylene (prosthetic graft)
RLN recurrent laryngeal nerve
SBP systolic blood pressure
SCM sternocleidomastoid (muscle)
SFA superficial femoral artery
SFJ saphenofemoral junction
SLN superior laryngeal nerve
SMA superior mesenteric artery
SSV small (short) saphenous vein
SVS Society for Vascular Surgery
TBI toe-brachial index
TcPO2 transcutaneous oxygen pressure
TCAR transcarotid artery revascularization
TEVAR thoracic endovascular aortic repair
TIA transient ischemic attack
TMA transmetatarsal amputation
US ultrasound
USPSTF U.S. Preventive Services Task Force
VAN Vein, Artery, Nerve (carotid sheath, lateral→medial)
VTE venous thromboembolism
WIfI Wound, Ischemia, foot Infection (SVS limb-threat classification)

Trials & studies

Trial Full name
ACAS Asymptomatic Carotid Atherosclerosis Study
ACST Asymptomatic Carotid Surgery Trial
ADAM Aneurysm Detection and Management (VA trial)
BASIL / BASIL-2 Bypass versus Angioplasty in Severe Ischaemia of the Leg
BEST-CLI Best Endovascular versus Best Surgical Therapy in CLTI
CLEVER Claudication: Exercise Versus Endoluminal Revascularization
COMPASS Cardiovascular Outcomes for People Using Anticoagulation Strategies
CREST Carotid Revascularization Endarterectomy versus Stenting Trial
CREST-2 second CREST trial: revascularization vs intensive medical therapy (2025)
DREAM Dutch Randomized Endovascular Aneurysm Management
ECST European Carotid Surgery Trial
EVAR-1 Endovascular Aneurysm Repair trial 1
NASCET North American Symptomatic Carotid Endarterectomy Trial
OVER Open Versus Endovascular Repair (VA trial)
UKSAT UK Small Aneurysm Trial
VOYAGER-PAD rivaroxaban + aspirin after lower-extremity revascularization (trial)
tap / space to flip
double-tap or pinch to zoom
